questions.question: What is the primary goal of processor affinity in a multiprocessor system, and what are its two main types?
keyPoints: The primary goal is to keep a process running on the same processor it was previously assigned to, to benefit from cached data.
The two main types are soft affinity (the scheduler tries but does not guarantee to keep a process on the same CPU) and hard affinity (a process can specify a set of CPUs it is allowed to run on).

questions.question: Compare and contrast symmetric multiprocessing (SMP) and asymmetric multiprocessing (ASMP) architectures, highlighting their key scheduling differences.
keyPoints: In SMP, all processors are peers; each runs an identical copy of the OS and can schedule its own processes, requiring synchronization for shared data structures in the scheduler.
In ASMP, a master-slave relationship exists; one master processor handles all scheduling decisions, I/O, and other system activities, while slave processors execute only user code.
SMP is more common and complex due to the need for synchronization, while ASMP is simpler but can create a bottleneck at the master processor.

questions.question: Explain how load balancing is achieved in a multiprocessor system and discuss the trade-offs between its two primary approaches: push migration and pull migration.
keyPoints: Load balancing is the process of distributing work evenly across all CPUs in a system to maximize processor utilization.
Push migration involves a specific task (e.g., a kernel thread) that periodically checks the load on each processor and redistributes processes by 'pushing' them from overloaded to underloaded CPUs.
Pull migration occurs when an idle processor proactively 'pulls' a waiting task from a busy processor's queue.

The trade-off: Push migration can be proactive but adds overhead due to periodic checks. Pull migration is reactive and on-demand, which can be more efficient but may not always prevent an initial load imbalance.
